2026-06-09T08:47:30.1616980Z [36;1mset -euo pipefail[0m
2026-06-09T08:47:30.1617417Z [36;1m# actions/download-artifact (>=v5) flattens contents directly into `path/`[0m
2026-06-09T08:47:30.1617945Z [36;1m# whenever exactly one artifact matches the pattern, ignoring[0m
2026-06-09T08:47:30.1618448Z [36;1m# `merge-multiple: false`. Re-shape into the per-group subdir layout so[0m
2026-06-09T08:47:30.1618971Z [36;1m# downstream validation sees a stable structure regardless of matrix size.[0m
2026-06-09T08:47:30.1619392Z [36;1m# Upstream reference:[0m
2026-06-09T08:47:30.1619846Z [36;1m# https://github.com/actions/download-artifact/blob/v8.0.1/src/download-artifact.ts[0m
2026-06-09T08:47:30.1620462Z [36;1m# (see the `isSingleArtifactDownload || mergeMultiple || artifacts.length === 1`[0m
2026-06-09T08:47:30.1621044Z [36;1m# branch). Remove this step once download-artifact stops flattening or[0m
2026-06-09T08:47:30.1621469Z [36;1m# exposes an opt-out.[0m
2026-06-09T08:47:30.1621841Z [36;1mexpected_count=$(echo "$EXPECTED_MATRIX" | jq '.group // [] | length')[0m
2026-06-09T08:47:30.1622584Z [36;1mif [ "$expected_count" -eq 1 ]; then[0m
2026-06-09T08:47:30.1622955Z [36;1m  group_id=$(echo "$EXPECTED_MATRIX" | jq -r '.group[0].id')[0m
2026-06-09T08:47:30.1623440Z [36;1m  # Defence-in-depth: group_id is interpolated into a shell path. apm-prep[0m
2026-06-09T08:47:30.1623958Z [36;1m  # produces a sanitised id today, but enforce a strict allowlist here so[0m
2026-06-09T08:47:30.1624444Z [36;1m  # any future schema drift cannot smuggle traversal sequences.[0m
2026-06-09T08:47:30.1624907Z [36;1m  if ! printf '%s' "$group_id" | grep -Eq '^[A-Za-z0-9_-]+$'; then[0m
2026-06-09T08:47:30.1625373Z [36;1m    echo "::error::unsafe group_id '$group_id' (must match ^[A-Za-z0-9_-]+$)"[0m
2026-06-09T08:47:30.1625768Z [36;1m    exit 1[0m
2026-06-09T08:47:30.1626003Z [36;1m  fi[0m
2026-06-09T08:47:30.1626343Z [36;1m  group_dir="/tmp/gh-aw/apm-bundles/${ARTIFACT_PREFIX}apm-${group_id}"[0m
2026-06-09T08:47:30.1626741Z [36;1m  if [ ! -d "$group_dir" ]; then[0m
2026-06-09T08:47:30.1627250Z [36;1m    mkdir -p "$group_dir"[0m
2026-06-09T08:47:30.1627739Z [36;1m    find /tmp/gh-aw/apm-bundles -mindepth 1 -maxdepth 1 ! -path "$group_dir" -exec mv {} "$group_dir/" \;[0m

2026-06-09T08:47:43.3805806Z [36;1m# Mask immediately to prevent timing vulnerabilities[0m
2026-06-09T08:47:43.3806236Z [36;1mAPI_KEY=$(openssl rand -base64 45 | tr -d '/+=')[0m
2026-06-09T08:47:43.3806591Z [36;1mecho "::add-mask::${API_KEY}"[0m
2026-06-09T08:47:43.3806875Z [36;1m[0m

The patch release shipping this fix should be framed explicitly as a 'safety release' in the GitHub release notes — not buried in a list of fixes. That one sentence converts a near-miss data-loss incident into a trust differentiator against competitors who ship installers without such guards. 2026-06-09T09:00:02.4190022Z     {
2026-06-09T09:00:02.4223865Z       "severity": "recommended",
2026-06-09T09:00:03.6087873Z       "summary": "TestSentinelInvariants presence-tests are unreachable precisely when they matter",
2026-06-09T09:00:08.0258071Z       "rationale": "_load_validator() is called at module scope (test_install_safety.py:54: `_VALIDATOR_SRC = _load_validator()`). If either sentinel is absent, the `assert match_end/begin is not None` inside _load_validator() raises AssertionError during collection, reporting a pytest collection error — not a test failure. TestSentinelInvariants.test_begin_sentinel_present (line 343) and test_end_sentinel_present (line 346) are therefore unreachable in the scenario where they would have diagnostic value: the module never imports. The only test in the class that adds unique signal beyond the module-level assert is test_function_defined_in_extracted_block (line 350), which verifies that the extracted block actually contains the function body rather than just that markers exist.\n\nThe practical risk: a refactor that strips the sentinels surfaces as 'ERROR collecting tests/unit/install/test_install_safety.py' rather than a clear sentinel-guard test failure with a named test ID. CI output is harder to bisect.",
2026-06-09T09:00:08.0264293Z       "file": "tests/unit/install/test_install_safety.py",
2026-06-09T09:00:08.0268269Z       "line": 337,
2026-06-09T09:00:11.6633338Z       "suggestion": "Move the sentinel-presence invariant into the local conftest.py as a session-scoped autouse fixture that calls pytest.fail() (not assert) when markers are missing. pytest.fail() inside a fixture surfaces as a named test failure with a descriptive message even during collection; assert inside module-scope code produces a confusing collection error. Example:\n\n@pytest.fixture(autouse=True, scope='session')\ndef _sentinel_invariants():\n    text = INSTALL_SH.read_text(encoding='utf-8')\n    if not SENTINEL_BEGIN.search(text):\n        pytest.fail('INSTALL_SAFETY_BEGIN sentinel missing in install.sh -- re-add to preserve testability')\n    if not SENTINEL_END.search(text):\n        pytest.fail('INSTALL_SAFETY_END sentinel missing in install.sh')\n\nRetain test_function_defined_in_extracted_block since it checks block contents, not just marker presence."
2026-06-09T09:00:11.6637425Z     },
2026-06-09T09:00:11.6637753Z     {
2026-06-09T09:00:11.6638097Z       "severity": "recommended",
2026-06-09T09:00:12.8597003Z       "summary": "Second apm_lib_dir_validate call before rm -rf carries a misleading error message; only guard 4 can change state between calls",
2026-06-09T09:00:18.4661124Z       "rationale": "The second call at install.sh:599 is defense-in-depth and logically sound — guard 4 (marker-file check) depends on directory state which CAN change between the two validation calls. However, guards 1-3 (absolute path, suffix, blocklist) operate on the immutable $APM_LIB_DIR string and will always produce the same result as the first call. The error message at line 601 — 'APM_LIB_DIR became unsafe before removal; refusing to delete' — implies that the path itself changed, which cannot happen. A maintainer who does not trace both call sites may read the second call as pure redundancy and remove it, silently losing the guard-4 re-check (the check that protects against a directory being created and filled by another process in the validation window).\n\nDesign patterns note:\n- Used in this PR: Chain of Responsibility (apm_lib_dir_validate guards 1->2->3->4, first failing guard returns rc 11-14, caller short-circuits); Sentinel-Bounded Extraction (# INSTALL_SAFETY_BEGIN/END delineates an extractable block for subprocess-level testing without splitting the file into separate sourced modules).\n- Pragmatic suggestion: none — the layered-guard design is the simplest correct shape for four ordered safety invariants at this scope; adding a strategy table or enum-mapped validator class would be over-engineering. The sentinel pattern is novel but well-suited: it keeps the validator co-located with the install logic while enabling Python-driven subprocess tests.",

2026-06-09T09:00:57.5118556Z     {
2026-06-09T09:00:57.5123583Z       "severity": "nit",
2026-06-09T09:00:58.5676556Z       "summary": "Guard 4: behavior when `ls -A` fails with permission denied is undocumented by test",
2026-06-09T09:01:03.9866379Z       "rationale": "Guard 4 in install.sh (line 551) checks `[ \"$(ls -A \"$_apm_lib_dir\" 2>/dev/null)\" ]`. When `ls -A` fails (e.g. the directory has mode 0o311—execute-only), the `2>/dev/null` silences the error and the substitution returns empty string. The guard then treats the directory as empty, short-circuits, and returns 0—allowing a subsequent `rm -rf` to proceed. No test creates a target directory with restrictive permissions and verifies the guard's behavior in that case. Probe: grep for 'chmod.*0o0\\|chmod.*0o1\\|ls.*A\\|unreadable' in test_install_safety.py → 0 matches. The TestUserLocalInstall.test_prepare_parent_falls_back_when_parent_unwritable test uses chmod 0o555 on a PARENT directory (for apm_prepare_lib_parent), not on the APM_LIB_DIR target itself. In practice this edge case requires the user to own a directory they cannot list, which is rare. Documenting the behavior with a test● Read (Python-architect agent — Python architect panel review)

2026-06-09T09:01:17.0847991Z </thinking>
2026-06-09T09:01:17.0848168Z

2026-06-09T09:01:17.7095558Z 4. **`readlink -f` through parent symlink chains** → correctly resolves to real target path on Linux (Guard 3 works)
2026-06-09T09:01:17.7096289Z 
2026-06-09T09:01:17.7097123Z ```json
2026-06-09T09:01:17.7097527Z {
2026-06-09T09:01:17.7105639Z   "persona": "supply-chain-security-expert",
2026-06-09T09:01:17.7116162Z   "active": true,
2026-06-09T09:01:19.7124832Z   "summary": "The four-guard design (absolute path, /apm suffix, blocklist exact-match, marker-file) is architecturally sound for the stated threat model of accidental data loss on rm -rf. Empirical testing confirms: (1) rm -rf on a path whose final component is a symlink removes only the symlink, never target contents — mitigating the most obvious symlink bypass; (2) Guard 4 (marker check) reliably catches the parent-symlink traversal case for non-empty, non-APM-marked directories; (3) the double-validation pattern prevents the most practical TOCTOU swap because the second check sees any blocklisted-target symlink before rm -rf runs. No blocking vulnerabilities found. Two recommended improvements: blocklist completeness and a macOS-specific readlink -f fallback gap for non-existent paths.",
2026-06-09T09:01:19.7129638Z   "findings": [
2026-06-09T09:01:19.7130106Z     {
2026-06-09T09:01:19.7132999Z       "severity": "recommended",
2026-06-09T09:01:19.7134236Z       "summary": "Blocklist missing high-value system directories: /etc, /var, /home, /root",
2026-06-09T09:01:22.1410923Z       "rationale": "The blocklist omits /etc, /var, /home (parent of all home dirs), and /root. A symlink where APM_LIB_DIR resolves exactly to one of these passes Guard 3. Guard 4 provides a reliable fallback (non-empty dirs without APM markers are rejected with return 14), so in practice data loss is still prevented for non-empty targets. However, defense-in-depth is the right posture: an explicit blocklist entry for /etc is a cheaper and more legible guard than relying solely on Guard 4. The missing entries cost nothing to add and close the explicit first-line catch before Guard 4 is even needed.",
2026-06-09T09:01:22.1413289Z       "file": "install.sh",
2026-06-09T09:01:23.3400507Z       "line": 533,
2026-06-09T09:01:23.3421410Z       "suggestion": "Add /etc, /var, /home, /root (and optionally /proc, /sys, /dev) to the APM_BLOCKLIST_EOF heredoc between /tmp and /."
2026-06-09T09:01:23.3425391Z     },
2026-06-09T09:01:23.3426058Z     {
2026-06-09T09:01:23.3426751Z       "severity": "recommended",
2026-06-09T09:01:23.3451707Z       "summary": "Guard 3 fails open for non-existent APM_LIB_DIR on macOS: unresolved path used for blocklist check",
2026-06-09T09:01:27.4824892Z       "rationale": "GNU readlink -f resolves symlinks in existing path components even when the final component does not yet exist (confirmed: exit 0 in testing). On macOS, readlink -f exits non-zero for any non-existent path component; realpath behaves the same way. For a fresh install where APM_LIB_DIR has never been created but a parent directory is a symlink — e.g., /opt/mytools/lib -> /real/lib, APM_LIB_DIR=/opt/mytools/lib/apm — macOS falls through to echo $_apm_lib_dir, so Guard 3 uses the unresolved /opt/mytools/lib/apm for the blocklist check rather than /real/lib/apm. Guard 4 does not apply because the directory does not exist yet. The installer proceeds, rm -rf is a no-op (path does not exist), and mkdir -p /opt/mytools/lib/apm silently creates the directory at /real/lib/apm — APM lands in an unexpected real location. This is installation misdirection rather than data loss, but is still a correctness failure in Guard 3's stated purpose of catching symlink bypasses.",
2026-06-09T09:01:27.9223714Z       "file": "install.sh",
2026-06-09T09:01:27.9224199Z       "line": 523,
2026-06-09T09:01:28.9352339Z       "suggestion": "Make resolution portable for non-existent final components by resolving the parent independently: _apm_lib_dir_real=\"$(readlink -f \"$(dirname \"$_apm_lib_dir\")\" 2>/dev/null || realpath \"$(dirname \"$_apm_lib_dir\")\" 2>/dev/null || dirname \"$_apm_lib_dir\")/$(basename \"$_apm_lib_dir\")\". The parent must exist or be creatable for the install to proceed anyway, so its canonical path can always be resolved. This approach is also correct on GNU/Linux and eliminates the platform difference."
2026-06-09T09:01:28.9355050Z     },
2026-06-09T09:01:28.9355609Z     {
2026-06-09T09:01:28.9355822Z       "severity": "nit",
2026-06-09T09:01:30.1103638Z       "summary": "TOCTOU window between first and second validations is real but impact-bounded; worth a comment",
2026-06-09T09:01:33.7289910Z       "rationale": "A local adversary with write access to the parent of APM_LIB_DIR could race a symlink swap between lines 573 and 599. In practice the second check (line 599) catches any swap whose resolved target is in the blocklist. And rm -rf on a path whose final component is a symlink (confirmed empirically: no trailing slash means only the symlink is removed, not the target directory) bounds worst-case impact even if the second check is somehow raced. A remaining risk scenario requires the attacker to have pre-seeded a .apm-installed marker in the swap target — which implies they already control that directory and could invoke rm themselves. Acceptable for an install script, but a comment helps future maintainers reason about the residual risk rather than assuming the double-check eliminates all TOCTOU exposure.",
2026-06-09T09:01:33.7294056Z       "file": "install.sh",
2026-06-09T09:01:33.7294434Z       "line": 597,
2026-06-09T09:01:33.7321829Z       "suggestion": "Add a comment: '# Second validation is defense-in-depth against TOCTOU symlink swap. Note: rm -rf on a symlink path (no trailing slash) removes only the symlink, not the target — bounding worst-case impact of any remaining race window.'"
2026-06-09T09:01:33.7324506Z     },
2026-06-09T09:01:33.7325020Z     {
2026-06-09T09:01:33.7331183Z       "severity": "nit",
2026-06-09T09:01:34.5127421Z       "summary": ".apm-installed marker is an unauthenticated heuristic; document it as such to prevent future confusion",
2026-06-09T09:01:35.7213365Z       "rationale": "Any process with write access to a directory can touch .apm-installed and bypass Guard 4. This is the correct design for the stated threat model (preventing accidental deletion of a non-APM directory by an ordinary user) and no stronger signal is needed. However, the absence of a clarifying comment risks a future maintainer treating the marker as a cryptographic integrity anchor and building higher-trust logic on it.",
2026-06-09T09:01:35.7217258Z       "file": "install.sh",
2026-06-09T09:01:35.7217549Z       "line": 549,
2026-06-09T09:01:36.9295965Z       "suggestion": "Add a comment before Guard 4: '# Guard 4 is a heuristic against accidental data loss, not a cryptographic integrity check. Any writer who controls the target directory can plant .apm-installed. The blocklist (Guard 3) is the primary protection against deliberate symlink-based misdirection.'"
2026-06-09T09:01:36.9297849Z     },
2026-06-09T09:01:36.9298139Z     {
2026-06-09T09:01:36.9299112Z       "severity": "nit",
2026-06-09T09:01:36.9332896Z       "summary": "trap single-quote fix is correct idiom; original double-quote form also worked correctly in context",
2026-06-09T09:01:39.3259702Z       "rationale": "The original trap \"rm -rf $TMP_DIR\" EXIT expanded $TMP_DIR at trap-registration time — which is technically correct here since TMP_DIR was set on the immediately preceding line and never reassigned. The new single-quoted form defers expansion to trap execution time (canonical POSIX recommendation) and eliminates the theoretical word-splitting risk if TMP_DIR ever contained whitespace (mktemp -d should not produce spaces, but the unquoted variable in a double-quoted trap command would split). The fix is an unambiguous improvement.",
2026-06-09T09:01:39.3263105Z       "file": "install.sh",
2026-06-09T09:01:39.3263505Z       "line": 317
2026-06-09T09:01:39.3263865Z     },
2026-06-09T09:01:39.3264153Z     {
2026-06-09T09:01:39.3266570Z       "severity": "nit",
2026-06-09T09:01:39.3305479Z       "summary": "echo -e in error messages is correct for #!/bin/bash but degrades when invoked via pipe to sh",
2026-06-09T09:01:43.8764533Z       "rationale": "The script shebang is #!/bin/bash so echo -e correctly interprets \\n escape sequences in the new error block (lines 575-584). However, the documented usage (curl -sSL ... | sh) pipes the script to sh directly, which ignores the shebang. On systems where sh is dash (Ubuntu) or another POSIX shell, echo -e prints a literal leading -e and leaves \\n sequences unexpanded, producing garbled error output. This is a pre-existing pattern throughout the file and not introduced by this PR, but the new multi-line error messages at line 579-582 do use \\n inside echo -e and would be the most visibly affected on POSIX sh.",
2026-06-09T09:01:43.8767182Z       "file": "install.sh",
2026-06-09T09:01:43.8789621Z       "line": 575,
2026-06-09T09:01:45.3755952Z       "suggestion": "Pre-existing issue: consider replacing echo -e \"...\n...\" patterns with printf '...\\n...' for POSIX portability, or explicitly document #!/bin/bash as a requirement rather than supporting sh invocation."
2026-06-09T09:01:45.3757204Z     }

2026-06-09T09:06:32.5178399Z      - Added `apm_lib_dir_validate()` function with 4 guards:
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5178735Z        1. Absolute path guard
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5179003Z        2. Suffix guard (`/apm` ending)
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5179349Z        3. Blocklist guard (rejects shared/broad directories)
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5179736Z        4. Marker-file guard (prevents deleting non-APM data)

2026-06-09T09:06:32.5180869Z      - Wrapped the safety logic in markers for easier testing
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5181305Z      - Creates a `.apm-installed` marker file after successful installation
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5181835Z      - Uses Unicode box-drawing characters for error messages, which could be problematic
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5182417Z

2026-06-09T09:06:32.5368257Z   **PR Summary:**
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5368561Z   - Fixes a data-loss bug in the Unix installer (issue #1690)
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5369364Z   - A user who set `APM_LIB_DIR=$HOME/.local/share` while trying to install to `$HOME/.local/bin` lost their Atuin history DB because the installer ran `rm -rf "$APM_LIB_DIR"` unconditionally
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5370117Z   - The fix adds 4 safety guards before any `rm -rf`
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5370457Z   - Updates docs with the new constraint
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5370757Z   - Adds 355 lines of regression tests
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5371412Z   - The changes span the installer script itself, documentation updates across two files, and a comprehensive test suite to prevent this from happening again
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5372246Z   
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5373095Z   From a growth perspective, this is significant because it directly addresses a trust issue—preventing data loss is critical for user retention and word-of-mouth reputation.
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5373793Z   
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5375013Z   However, there's a notable gap: the CHANGELOG doesn't have an entry for this fix, which is a missed opportunity to signal to users that we've addressed a real data-loss bug. 2026-06-09T09:06:32.5376277Z

2026-06-09T09:06:32.5377885Z

The `TestSentinelInvariants` tests would catch that, but the pattern itself needs to be documented to prevent the issue in the first place.

2026-06-09T09:06:32.5385406Z   
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5386683Z   1. **Blocklist returns exit code 13, but tests assert `!= 0` and one incorrectly asserts `== 12`**: Looking at the test `test_rejects_broad_paths`, it asserts `!= 0` (correct). But looking at `test_rejects_local_share`, it asserts `== 12` (suffix guard fires first). However, looking at the test comments - the assert is correct for that path because suffix guard fires first (`.local/share` doesn't end in `/apm`).
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5387994Z      
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5389401Z      Actually, I need to reconsider the exit codes here. The blocklist guard returns 13, but paths like `/home/safe-user`, `/home/safe-user/.local`, and `/home/safe-user/.local/share` would hit the suffix guard first since they don't end in `/apm`, returning code 12. Only paths like `/usr`, `/opt`, `/tmp`, and `/` would also fail the suffix check, but they'd still return 12 before reaching the blocklist logic.
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5390692Z   
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5395040Z      This means the test suite labeled `TestBlocklistGuard` is actually mostly testing the suffix guard, not the blocklist guard itself—the blocklist exit code 13 is never actually triggered by these tests. The blocklist guard would only fire if someone set `APM_LIB_DIR` to an exact blocklist path that somehow ends in `/apm`, which doesn't match any standard blocklist entries. There's a potential edge case with symlinks, but I'm realizing the test coverage here has a significant gap. The blocklist guard resolves both the actual library directory and each blocklist entry to their real paths using `readlink -f`, then compares them for equality. For the guard to trigger and return the error code, the resolved path of `$APM_LIB_DIR` would need to match the resolved path of a blocklist entry, and it would also need to pass the suffix check first. `$HOME` blocklist entry and return 13. I'm realizing there's actually no test coverage for this symlink bypass scenario where `APM_LIB_DIR` resolves to a blocklisted path. The comparison uses resolved pa... 2026-06-09T09:06:32.5405050Z   
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5405250Z   ## Key Findings
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5405464Z   
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5405723Z   ### 1. Unicode Box-Drawing Characters (CRITICAL/BLOCKING)
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5406047Z   
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5406349Z   The new error block at lines 575-584 uses Unicode box-drawing characters:
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5406976Z   - `╔` (U+2554), `║` (U+2551), `╠` (U+2560), `╚` (U+255A), `═` (U+2550), `╗` (U+2557), `╣` (U+2563), `╝` (U+255D)
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5410525Z   These violate the repository's strict ASCII-only constraint and will trigger `UnicodeEncodeError` on Windows terminals with cp1252 encoding. While `install.sh` is a Unix script, the risk extends to macOS/Linux systems with non-UTF-8 locales or when output is piped through codec-limited tools. The repository enforces ASCII across all source files, making this a clear violation. The pre-existing banner violates this same rule, but since it's already in the codebase, the new error block introduced by this PR is the actual violation. Beyond the encoding issue, the error messages are being sent to stdout instead of stderr, which breaks the convention for error output in scripts. The new code also doesn't follow the project's STATUS_SYMBOLS pattern—it uses Unicode box-drawing characters instead of the standard `[x]` error prefix format that the rest of the script uses.
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5413577Z   
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5414433Z   There's also a portability concern with `echo -e`, which isn't supported in `/bin/sh` on some systems like macOS, though this is a pre-existing pattern throughout the script that the PR is expanding on.
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5415190Z   
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5416542Z   Looking at the four error codes, I'm analyzing how clearly each one communicates the problem. The relative path error (exit code 11) and the missing `/apm` suffix error (exit code 12) both explain what's wrong, though they could be more specific about the actual fix or show the current value more prominently in the message itself. Good: includes the exact command to fix it, has a safety warning, and is very actionable.
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5417924Z   
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5418553Z   The main issues I'm seeing are the Unicode box-drawing characters violating the repo's ASCII constraint, error messages going ... [truncated: showing first 2000 of 4852 chars]
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5419142Z 
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5419354Z [163] ✓ $ head -5 /home/runner/work/apm/apm/install.sh
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5419578Z 
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5419869Z [164] ✓ view(path: /home/runner/work/apm/apm/install.sh, view_range: [570, 610])
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5420173Z 
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5420613Z [165] ✓ $ which pytest || find /opt /usr -name pytest 2>/dev/null | head -10; find /home/runner -name pytest 2>/dev/null | head -5
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5421037Z 
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5421366Z [166] ✓ $ grep -n "readlink\|realpath\|symlink" /home/runner/work/apm/apm/install.sh | head -20
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5421706Z 
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5421858Z [167] ◐ Now I have a full picture:
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5422298Z   
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5424606Z   1. **Link fix in quickstart.mdx** - The PR changed from `./getting-started/installation/` to `../getting-started/installation/`. Both `quickstart.mdx` and the `getting-started/` directory are at the same level: `docs/src/content/docs/`. So from `quickstart.mdx`, the correct relative path to `getting-started/installation/` would be `./getting-started/installation/` (or just `getting-started/installation/`). The `../getting-started/installation/` would go UP from `docs/src/content/docs/` to `docs/src/content/` and then try to find `getting-started/` there — which doesn't exist. This is **wrong**. 
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5426528Z   
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5430998Z   Actually, I should reconsider how Starlight handles relative links. Different SSG frameworks process them differently, and Starlight might have its own conventions for how it resolves paths in content files. The URL structure that Starlight generates might not map directly to the file system structure the way I'm thinking about it. Starlight resolves links based on the rendered URL path, not the file system structure. So from a page at `/quickstart/`, a relative link like `./getting-started/installation/` would incorrectly resolve to `/quickstart/getting-started/installation/`, whereas `../getting-started/installation/` would correctly resolve to `/getting-started/installation/`. Let me think through this more carefully to make sure I have the right behavior. page at `/apm/quickstart/` with a trailing slash means we're conceptually inside that directory, so `./getting-started/installation/` would resolve to `/apm/quickstart/getting-started/installation/` (wrong), while `../getting-started/installation/` goes up to `/apm/` and then into `getting-started/installation/` (correct). So the PR's change is actually fixing a broken link. Though I should verify how Starlight specifically handles relative links in markdown to be sure. not the file-system path. I'm second-guessing myself on how Starlight actually handles relative links, so let me look at th... [truncated: showing first 2000 of 6278 chars]

2026-06-09T09:06:32.5442213Z   1. The shebang is `#!/bin/bash`, not `#!/bin/sh`. So `echo -e` portability to dash is less of a concern. However, the usage instructions say `curl -sSL https://aka.ms/apm-unix | sh` which would use `/bin/sh`. On systems where `/bin/sh` is bash (most systems), this works. On systems where `/bin/sh` is dash (Debian/Ubuntu), this could be an issue. But since the script uses `#!/bin/bash`, when run directly it's bash. When piped via `curl ... | sh`, the shebang is ignored and `/bin/sh` is used. But wait, the comment says "curl -sSL https://aka.ms/apm-unix | sh" — so when piped to sh, the shebang is ignored, and sh is likely bash on most systems, but on Debian/Ubuntu, sh is dash.
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5444689Z   2. The curl-pipe approach bypasses the shebang and uses `/bin/sh`, which creates a real compatibility issue, though this is pre-existing and not introduced by this PR. I'll flag it as a nit.

2026-06-09T09:06:32.5513586Z   
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5513829Z   1. **Guard Design / `/apm` suffix requirement**:
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5514261Z      - The suffix requirement (`/apm` only) is quite narrow and defensively designed.
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5515074Z      - The key question: Does this break existing custom installs that had `APM_LIB_DIR=/some/custom/dir` (without `/apm` suffix)?
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5515812Z      - The tests show that `/opt/apm` and `/home/user/.local/lib/apm` are the kinds of paths that pass. 
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5516444Z      - The suffix guard catches the original incident (`$HOME/.local/share` does not end in `/apm`).
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5518216Z      - This suffix constraint is actually a breaking change—any user who previously set a custom `APM_LIB_DIR` without the `/apm` ending would now be blocked on upgrade. But looking at the documentation and defaults, `APM_LIB_DIR` was never really meant to be customized freely; the default always ends in `/apm`, so this constraint likely only affects edge cases or undocumented usage patterns.
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5520581Z      - The error message would tell users to rename their path, but that's not a clean migration path since renaming means losing the old install's files—the suffix check fires before the marker guard that would normally protect non-empty directories. So someone with `/some/custom/myapmdir` would hit a hard block.
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5522387Z      - This is a real concern for users upgrading via simple shell one-liners if they happen to have `APM_LIB_DIR` set to something non-standard, though realistically I'm wondering how many users would actually have done this.

2026-06-09T09:06:32.5563772Z      - After symlink resolution, equals a blocklist entry
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5564088Z      
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5564631Z      All tests in `TestBlocklistGuard` use paths that don't end in `/apm`, so the suffix guard blocks them first. The blocklist guard itself is untested.
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5565213Z   
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5566098Z   2. **Symlink traversal scenarios aren't covered**: The blocklist guard specifically uses `readlink -f` to resolve symlinks, but there's no test for a case where someone creates `/malicious/path/apm` as a symlink pointing to `$HOME/.local/share` to bypass the check.
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5567043Z   
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5567695Z   3. **The full install.sh flow isn't tested end-to-end**: The tests only validate the extracted validation function in isolation, not how it integrates with the actual installation script.
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5568529Z   
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5569356Z   4. **Import-time failures mask test coverage**: If the sentinel files are missing, `_load_validator()` fails during module import before `TestSentinelInvariants` can run, so those tests become unreachable even though the failure itself is loud.
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5570231Z   
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5571278Z   5. **Permission denied on `ls -A` silently allows deletion**: The code redirects stderr to `/dev/null` when checking if a directory is empty, so a permission error gets masked and the directory appears empty, allowing unsafe deletion—this is a security issue.
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5572307Z   
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5573366Z   Looking at severity, the blocklist guard's exit code 13 is completely untested despite being the symlink traversal defense, making this a recommended finding since the main suffix guard does provide protection. The missing symlink bypass test is another gap in cov... [truncated: showing first 2000 of 3731 chars]

2026-06-09T09:06:32.5577422Z [205] ◐ Now I have all the information I need to write my final JSON response. Let me synthesize my findings.
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5577895Z   
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5578088Z   Key UX findings:
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5578332Z   
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5579233Z   1. **Error code 12 migration gap** (recommended): When a user with a non-`/apm`-suffix `APM_LIB_DIR` upgrades, they get error 12 but no guidance on what happened to their old install (it's still there, untouched). The message should tell them their existing files were NOT touched.
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5580186Z   
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5580881Z   2. **Error code 13 example suggests sudo-requiring path** (nit): The suggestion to use `/usr/local/lib/apm` is sudo-requiring; it should instead show the INSTALL_DIR derivation pattern that avoids sudo.
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5583045Z   3. **Suffix guard breaks existing custom installs during upgrades** (recommended): Users who previously set `APM_LIB_DIR` without the `/apm` suffix (like `/opt/myapmbundle`) can't re-run the installer to upgrade because the suffix check blocks them before the marker check can recognize their old install as valid. This is a breaking change for intentional custom setups, not just user error, but the PR doesn't flag it as such.
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5584478Z   
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5585610Z   4. **Callout placement is suboptimal**: The warning about `APM_LIB_DIR` validation lives under the "Permission denied" troubleshooting section, but users who intentionally customized `APM_LIB_DIR` are the ones most likely to hit these errors and probably won't look there. The table row addition is solid, but the placement could be better.
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5586762Z   
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5587336Z   The quickstart link fix checks out, the default behavior works fine through all the guards, and the parent directory creation logic prevents certain edge cases.
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5587966Z   
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5588792Z   The trap statement fix handles spaces in paths correctly, error code 14 provides the exact recovery command, and error codes 11 and 12 have concrete examples. The conftest.py setup properly isolates shell script tests from Python dependencies.

2026-06-09T09:06:32.5609994Z [212] ◐ Now let me analyze the PR in detail:
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5610288Z   
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5610531Z   1. **Shell script function design patterns**: 
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5611385Z      - `apm_lib_dir_validate()` - Clean Chain of Responsibility pattern with ordered guards (11→12→13→14). First failing guard wins, returning early. This is the right approach.
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5612215Z      - `apm_prepare_lib_parent()` - Simple utility function.
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5612702Z      - The global variable pollution is handled with `_apm_` prefix on all locals - good.
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5613268Z   
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5613556Z   2. **The sentinel-bounded extraction pattern for testability**: 
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5614059Z      - The `# INSTALL_SAFETY_BEGIN` / `# INSTALL_SAFETY_END` markers are an elegant solution. 
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5614477Z   
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5614758Z   3. **The test harness design (Python driving shell functions)**:
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5615679Z      - Tests extract the marked section via regex, source it into a fresh bash environment, then invoke the function. The validator source loads at import time with a sentinel assertion that fast-fails if the markers are missing.
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5616630Z      - `TestSentinelInvariants` directly validates the markers exist.
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5617243Z      - The subprocess call uses `bash -c` with a `--` separator to safely pass the lib directory without flag interpretation.
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5618033Z      - Environment injection sets a controlled HOME for blocklist tests, and a 10-second timeout guards against hanging processes.
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5618835Z      - Temporary directories stage the test files, and the function returns only the exit code to keep the interface clean.
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5619346Z   
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5619578Z   4. **The `conftest.py` override pattern**:
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5620110Z      - Overrides exist because autouse fixtures from the root conftest would import `apm_cli`, which we need to avoid.
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5621083Z      - The `_validate_primitive_coverage` fixture uses session scope to match the root's scope, while `_isolate_discovery_state` is function-scoped with a yield statement for consistency.
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5621780Z   
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5622032Z   5. **Global variable pollution in shell functions**:
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5622857Z      - The `_apm_` prefix convention is consistently applied across functions like `apm_lib_dir_validate` and `apm_prepare_lib_parent` to avoid collisions in POSIX shell.
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5623930Z      - Since the script uses POSIX `sh` compatibility, `local` declarations aren't available, so the prefix strategy is the righ... [truncated: showing first 2000 of 15459 chars]
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5624507Z

2026-06-09T09:06:32.5626486Z 
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5628442Z [216] ◐ Key observation: The script uses `#!/bin/bash` (not `#!/bin/sh`). This means `local` variables ARE available but the PR chose the `_apm_` prefix approach instead. This is a noteworthy observation - in bash, `local` is the correct way to scope variables to a function. Using `_apm_` prefix at the global scope without `local` means these variables leak into the script's global variable namespace. In bash scripts with `set -e`, this could cause subtle issues if the function is called multiple times (variables from a previous call would persist). 
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5630198Z   
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5630722Z   However, looking at the current script, `apm_lib_dir_validate` is called twice (lines 573 and 599). The variables `_apm_lib_dir`, `_apm_safe`, etc. 
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5631299Z   
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5632509Z   There's an inconsistency here: the shebang declares bash, which supports `local`, but the implementation uses the `_apm_` prefix pattern typically chosen for POSIX `sh` compatibility. The usage instructions mention piping through `sh`, which explains the design choice, but it conflicts with the actual shebang declaration.
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5633633Z   
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5635100Z   The real issue is that the `_apm_` variables do leak into the global namespace after functions execute, which is a genuine side effect worth addressing. The heredoc doesn't quote its marker, so `$HOME` gets expanded by the shell before the blocklist is created. This means if `HOME` is empty, it becomes a blank line that the loop skips over safely. But if `HOME` contains spaces, the path would be split across multiple lines, which could break the comparison logic.
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5636755Z   
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5637854Z   Actually, wait—the `read -r _apm_dir` reads the entire line including spaces, so `_apm_dir` would correctly capture `/home/my user` as a single value. With proper quoting in the `readlink -f` call and the comparison, spaces in `HOME` are handled correctly. So guard 3 is solid.
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5638798Z   
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5639681Z   Now I'm thinking through another edge case with the `while IFS= read -r _apm_dir` loop combined with the heredoc and `_apm_` namespace. After the loop finishes, `_apm_dir` and `_apm_dir_real` retain the values from the last non... [truncated: showing first 2000 of 3408 chars]

2026-06-09T09:06:32.5643001Z 
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5643341Z [220] ◐ Now I have a complete picture of the code. Let me analyze each of the security concerns:
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5643764Z   
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5643977Z   ### 1. Symlink bypass analysis
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5644228Z   
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5644449Z   The blocklist resolves symlinks via:
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5644722Z   ```sh
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5645166Z   _apm_lib_dir_real="$(readlink -f "$_apm_lib_dir" 2>/dev/null || realpath "$_apm_lib_dir" 2>/dev/null || echo "$_apm_lib_dir")"
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5645669Z   ```
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5645869Z   
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5646081Z   And for each blocklist entry:
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5646345Z   ```sh
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5646733Z   _apm_dir_real="$(readlink -f "$_apm_dir" 2>/dev/null || realpath "$_apm_dir" 2>/dev/null || echo "$_apm_dir")"
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5647190Z   ```
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5647384Z   
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5648458Z   The validation then compares the resolved real paths to check if `APM_LIB_DIR` matches any blocklisted directory. But there's a critical gap: if someone sets `APM_LIB_DIR` to a symlink pointing to a location that isn't explicitly in the blocklist, it would bypass the check entirely.
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5649448Z   
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5654397Z   For example, a symlink like `$HOME/.local/lib/apm` → `/home/user/.config` would pass the suffix guard and absolute path check, and when resolved, it would match the blocklist entry for `$HOME/.config`, so the validation would correctly catch it. But the broader issue is that the blocklist can't cover every sensitive directory an attacker might target. The actual comparison in the code is an exact equality check, not a prefix match. So a symlink pointing to `/home/user/.config/foo` wouldn't be caught by a blocklist entry for `/home/user/.config` since they're not equal strings. This reveals the real vulnerability: the blocklist only blocks exact matches with sensitive directories, not paths that exist *inside* those directories. ...but actually, the `rm -rf` only targets the exact directory specified, not its parent, so `/etc/apm` deletion wouldn't cascade upward. The real vulnerability is if `APM_LIB_DIR` points to a symlink—say `/some/path/apm` symlinks to `/home/user/documents`—then the guards would check the symlink path itself, not what it resolves to, potentially allowing deletion of unintended directories. I need to reconsider how `rm -rf` behaves with symlinks—when you run it on a symlink path without a trailing slash, it removes the symli... [truncated: showing first 2000 of 19010 chars]
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5658670Z

2026-06-09T09:06:32.5673184Z

2026-06-09T09:06:32.5676705Z

2026-06-09T09:06:32.5678247Z 
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5678694Z [226] ✓ $ cd /home/runner/work/apm/apm && grep -n '_validate_primitive\|_isolate_discovery' tests/conftest.py | head -10
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5679109Z 
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5679385Z [227] ✓ $ cd /home/runner/work/apm/apm && awk 'NR>=96 && NR<=120' tests/conftest.py
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5679679Z 
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5680145Z [228] ◐ Key observation: The root `conftest.py` imports `apm_cli` **inside the fixture function** (not at module level), which means:
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5680970Z   1. The `import apm_cli` only happens when the fixture is called, not at conftest load time
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5681716Z   2. The local `conftest.py` override with no-op fixtures WILL work correctly because the override prevents the fixture from being called at all
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5682404Z   
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5683441Z   This is good design. The local `conftest.py` correctly overrides both `_validate_primitive_coverage` (session scope) and `_isolate_discovery_state` (function scope) with no-ops. Since the `apm_cli` imports are inside the fixture functions (not module-level), the override prevents the import from happening.
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5688550Z   Now I'm thinking through how pytest resolves fixtures when tests run together in the same session. The session-scoped fixture from the root conftest might still execute for other tests in that session, which would trigger the import, but that's acceptable since the local override takes precedence for tests in the install directory. Let me trace through pytest's fixture resolution order more carefully—it checks the test file first, then the local conftest, then parent conftest files up the hierarchy. The real issue is that session-scoped fixtures get cached across the entire test session, so if the local no-op version runs first for the install tests, pytest might reuse that cached result for other tests expecting the actual validation logic, depending on how pytest resolves fixture identity across different conftest scopes. Session-scoped fixtures from a subdirectory conftest only cache within that conftest's scope, so there's no cross-contamination issue. Since the imports are inside the fixture functions and the local conftest properly overrides with no-ops, this approach should work reliably.
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5692381Z

2026-06-09T09:06:32.5707295Z

2026-06-09T09:06:32.5709086Z   
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5709677Z   This is the key finding: if a PARENT directory component of `APM_LIB_DIR` is a symlink, `rm -rf` WILL follow through it and delete the real directory at the resolved path.

The `rm -rf` succeeded in my test because I ran it directly without triggering the validation first—if the validation had been called, it would have blocked the operation at Guard 4.

2026-06-09T09:06:32.5729785Z

2026-06-09T09:06:32.5731725Z

2026-06-09T09:06:32.5733525Z   
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5735033Z   The site has a base of `/apm/` and always adds trailing slashes, so pages are at `/apm/quickstart/`, `/apm/getting-started/installation/`, etc. With `starlightLinksValidator` set to not error on relative links, it still validates them but won't fail the build. The real question is how Starlight resolves relative markdown links—whether it's relative to the file path or the URL path.
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5736315Z   
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5737775Z   In Starlight, relative links in content files are resolved relative to the **file path**, not the URL. So from `docs/src/content/docs/quickstart.mdx`, a link like `./getting-started/installation/` correctly resolves to the file at `docs/src/content/docs/getting-started/installation.md`, but `../getting-started/installation/` would try to go up a directory and fail. This means the PR's change from `./` to `../` is actually breaking the link resolution.
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5739258Z

2026-06-09T09:06:32.5764032Z [247] ◐ Key findings from the tests:
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5764300Z   
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5766189Z   1. **Parent symlink traversal**: `readlink -f $tmp/fake_path/lib/apm` → returns the RESOLVED path `$tmp/real_dir/apm` (exit 0). This means on Linux, `readlink -f` DOES correctly resolve through parent symlinks even when the final component doesn't exist (in fact it works when the final component does exist, as confirmed by the first test, and even when it doesn't in some cases). So on Linux, Guard 3 will correctly resolve the full path including parent symlinks.
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5767665Z

2026-06-09T09:06:32.5768895Z   
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5769865Z   So `readlink -f` resolves through the parent symlink and gives us the canonical path `/tmp/.../real_dir/apm`. 2026-06-09T09:06:32.5770870Z   
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5772502Z   For `rm -rf` on a last-component symlink without a trailing slash, it's safe—only the symlink gets removed. But when `rm -rf` goes through a parent symlink, that's the dangerous case: the earlier test showed `rm -rf "$tmp/fake_path/lib/apm"` actually deleted `$tmp/real_dir/apm`. Guard 4 would have blocked this since the directory was non-empty with no APM markers, so the system is protected there.
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5773810Z

2026-06-09T09:06:32.5775598Z   
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5776293Z   The real threat is non-empty directories, and Guard 4 handles that by requiring APM markers. For empty directories, deletion through parent symlinks is possible but harmless since no data is lost.

2026-06-09T09:06:32.5813749Z 
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5813908Z [258] ◐ Now I have definitive confirmation:
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5814265Z   - `docs/src/content/getting-started/` does NOT exist
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5814628Z   - `docs/src/content/docs/getting-started/` DOES exist
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5814936Z   
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5816024Z   So the PR's link change from `./getting-started/installation/` to `../getting-started/installation/` is wrong under file-relative resolution (which the codebase uses, as evidenced by other links in the getting-started files). The `../` in quickstart.mdx would point to `docs/src/content/getting-started/` which doesn't exist.
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5817128Z   
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5817582Z   However, `starlightLinksValidator` with `errorOnRelativeLinks: false` means this won't be caught at build time. 
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5818095Z   
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5819649Z   But there's another angle to consider: if Starlight actually uses URL-relative resolution instead, then the old link `./getting-started/installation/` from `/apm/quickstart/` would resolve to `/apm/quickstart/getting-started/installation/` (broken), while the new link `../getting-started/installation/` would resolve to `/apm/getting-started/installation/` (correct). So the fix depends entirely on which resolution method Starlight uses.
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5821261Z   
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5823348Z   Looking at the patterns in other files, file-relative resolution seems to be the standard, which means the PR actually introduced a broken link. The `starlightLinksValidator` won't catch this since it has `errorOnRelativeLinks: false`, so a broken link could slip through to production and cause 404s for users. Since I can't definitively verify the resolution behavior without running the site, I'm hesitant to call this a hard blocker, but I should recommend using an absolute path to eliminate the ambiguity entirely.
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5825064Z   
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5826442Z   Actually, I just realized I can check this definitively by looking at how other links in quickstart.mdx are written — they all use absolute paths like `/apm/...`. The PR's relative link is the only exception, which strongly suggests the original was correct and the PR broke it. The safest fix is to use an absolute path consistently with the rest of the file.
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5827629Z

2026-06-09T09:06:32.5935880Z [275] ✓ read_agent(agent_id: supply-chain-panelist, wait: true, timeout: 30)
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5936174Z 
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5936328Z [276] ◐ This is a very important finding!
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5936602Z   
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5936805Z   In the third test: 
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5937200Z   - `/fake/lib/apm` → symlink to `/dangerous_dir` (via parent symlink chain)
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5937895Z   - `readlink -f /fake/lib/apm` → resolves to `/tmp/.../dangerous_dir`!
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5938471Z   - `rm -rf /fake/lib/apm` → removes the symlink `/real/apm` only, dangerous_dir is SAFE!
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5938877Z   
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5939343Z   So when the final component IS a symlink (even through parent symlinks), `rm -rf` removes only the symlink, not the target.
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5939860Z   
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5940334Z   This is consistent with the earlier finding: `rm -rf` on a path where the final component is a symlink removes only the symlink.
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5942211Z   Guard 3 would check the resolved path `/tmp/dangerous_dir` against the blocklist, and since it's not blocked, it passes. Then Guard 4 examines whether `/fake/lib/apm` following symlinks is non-empty and lacks APM markers—if dangerous_dir has files but no markers, Guard 4 returns 14 (BLOCKED). Even if Guard 4 somehow passes, the deletion would still proceed.
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5943686Z   
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5945507Z   So the key insight is that when the final path component is a symlink, `rm -rf` only removes the symlink itself, not the target directory's contents. But when a parent component is a symlink and the final component is a real directory, `rm -rf` does delete the real directory—though Guard 4 protects against non-empty, non-APM directories. I'm building out a protection matrix to show how different scenarios interact with the guards and what `rm -rf` actually removes.
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5947288Z   
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5948988Z   The real vulnerability gap is parent symlink traversal where the final real directory is either empty or contains an APM marker—in those cases, the directory gets deleted. For empty directories there's no data loss, but if an attacker plants a marker, they've already got write access. Overall the security design holds up well, though improving blocklist completeness and handling macOS `readlink -f` behavior would strengthen it further.
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5950460Z   
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5951248Z   Now I'm compiling the final JSON report with my findings. The key insight is that Guard 3's symlink resolution works correctly even through parent symlink chains, properly identifying ... [truncated: showing first 2000 of 5596 chars]
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5952012Z 
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5952509Z [277] ◆ I now have all the evidence needed for a complete, empirically grounded review.
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5952925Z   
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5953122Z   Key results from testing:
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5953609Z   1. **`rm -rf` on a symlink (final component)** → removes only the symlink, not the target (SAFE)
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5954458Z   2. **`rm -rf` through a parent symlink to a real directory** → deletes the real directory (DANGEROUS, but Guard 4 catches non-empty, non-APM dirs)
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5955420Z   3. **GNU `readlink -f` on non-existent final paths** → exit 1 on Linux (falls back to unresolved path); on macOS even more reliably fails
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5956293Z   4. **`readlink -f` through parent symlink chains** → correctly resolves to real target path on Linux (Guard 3 works)
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5956783Z   
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5956971Z   ```json
2026-06-09T09:06:32.5957160Z   {

Empirical testing confirms: (1) rm -rf on a path whose final component is a symlink removes only the symlink, never target contents — mitigating the most obvious symlink bypass; (2) Guard 4 (marker check) reliably catches the parent-symlink traversal case for non-empty, non-APM-marked directories; (3) the double-validation pattern prevents the most practical TOCTOU swap because the second check sees any blocklisted-target symlink before rm -rf runs. Use with pagination parameters to control the number of results returned.\n 5. get_review_comments - Get review threads on a pull request. Each thread contains logically grouped review comments made on the same code location during pull request reviews. 2026-06-09T09:06:32.8147170Z {"timestamp":"2026-06-09T08:48:20.464Z","event":"rpc_request","_schema":"rpc-message/v2","direction":"OUT","server_id":"github","method":"tools/call","payload":{"jsonrpc":"2.0","method":"tools/call","params":{"arguments":{"method":"get","owner":"microsoft","pullNumber":"1694","repo":"apm"},"name":"pull_request_read"}}}
2026-06-09T09:06:32.8163374Z {"timestamp":"2026-06-09T08:48:21.368Z","event":"rpc_response","_schema":"rpc-message/v2","direction":"IN","server_id":"github","payload":{"id":1,"jsonrpc":"2.0","result":{"content":[{"text":"{\"number\":1694,\"title\":\"fix(install): validate APM_LIB_DIR before rm -rf to prevent data loss (closes #1690)\",\"body\":\"## Summary\\n\\nAdds four safety guards that run before `install.sh` deletes `APM_LIB_DIR`, fix the broken Quickstart Installation link, and document the new requirements. 32 pytest regression tests included.\\n\\n## Problem\\n\\n`install.sh` exposes `APM_LIB_DIR` as an environment override and unconditionally runs `rm -rf \\u0026#34;$APM_LIB_DIR\\u0026#34;`. A user who set `APM_LIB_DIR=$HOME/.local/share` while installing to `$HOME/.local/bin` lost unrelated application data (Atuin\\u0026#39;s local history DB). The Quickstart\\u0026#39;s `Installation` link is also a 404: `./getting-started/installation/` resolves to `/apm/quickstart/getting-started/installation/` instead of `/apm/getting-started/installation/`.\\n\\nCloses #1690.\\n\\n## Approach\\n\\nFour guards, ordered so the cheapest checks fire first:\\n\\n| Guard | Check | rc | Failure example |\\n|-------|-------|-----|-----------------|\\n| 1. Absolute path | Must start with `/` | 11 | `./apm` |\\n| 2. Suffix | Must end with `/apm` or `/lib/apm` | 12 | `$HOME/.local/share` (the reported incident) |\\n| 3. Blocklist | Must not be an exact match for a shared system directory (resolved via `readlink -f`/`realpath` to catch symlinks) | 13 | `$HOME`, `/usr`, `/opt`, `/tmp`, `/` |\\n| 4. Marker file | If the directory exists and is non-empty, at least one of `apm`/`apm.cmd`/`VERSION`/`.apm-installed` must be present | 14 | non-empty dir with random files |\\n\\nThe blocklist checks for **exact path matches only** (resolved). A path like `$HOME/apm` is allowed because the user has explicitly named an APM-specific directory. Suffix guard is the primary defence against accidentally broad paths.\\n\\n## Implementation\\n\\n- **`install.sh`**: Extracted guards into `apm_lib_dir_validate()` with `INSTALL_SAFETY_BEGIN`/`INSTALL_SAFETY_END` sentinel markers so tests can source the function without running the full installer.\\n- **`tests/unit/install/test_install_safety.py`**: 32 regression tests that source the extracted function in a fresh bash subprocess. No Python build dependencies needed — `conftest.py` shadows the autouse fixtures from `tests/conftest.py`.\\n- **Docs**: Fixed the Quickstart link and documented APM_LIB_DIR requirements in the installation page table and troubleshooting section.\\n\\n## Trade-offs\\n\\n- **Blocklist is exact-match**: `/home/user/apm` passes (suffix guard alone). A prefix-blocklist (e.g., \\u0026#34;any child of $HOME\\u0026#34;) would accidentally reject `$HOME/.local/lib/apm`, which is the documented safe path. Future hardening could add a separate prefix blacklist.\\n- **macOS readlink**: BSD `readlink` lacks `-f`. Falls back to `realpath`, then raw path. Suffix guard still blocks the reported incident on macOS regardless.\\n\\n## How to test\\n\\n```bash\\n# Run the regression suite\\npython3 -m pytest tests/unit/install/test_install_safety.py -v\\n\\n# Manual: the reported command should now fail safely (in a sandbox, no sudo)\\nbash -c \\u0026#39;\\n  source \\u0026lt;(sed -n \\u0026#34;/^# INSTALL_SAFETY_BEGIN/,/^# INSTALL_SAFETY_END/p\\u0026#34; install.sh)\\n  apm_lib_dir_validate \\u0026#34;$HOME/.local/share\\u0026#34; \\u0026amp;\\u0026amp; echo \\u0026#34;PASS\\u0026#34; || echo \\u0026#34;BLOCKED (rc=$?)\\u0026#34;\\n\\u0026#39;\\n```\",\"state\":\"open\",\"draft\":false,\"merged\":false,\"mergeable_state\":\"blocked\",\"html_url\":\"https://github.com/microsoft/apm/pull/1694\",\"user\":{\"login\":\"dohwi\",\"id\":78037515,\"profile_url\":\"https://github.com/dohwi\",\"avatar_url\":\"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/78037515?v=4\"},\"labels\":[\"panel-review\"],\"requested_reviewers\":[\"danielmeppiel\",\"sergio-sisternes-epam\"],\"head\":{\"ref\":\"fix/1690-installer-safety\",\"sha\":\"[REDACTED]\",\"repo\":{\"full_name\":\"dohwi/apm\",\"description\":\"Agent Package Manager\"}},\"base\":{\"ref\":\"main\",\"sha\":\"[REDACTED]\",\"repo\":{\"full_name\":\"microsoft/apm\",\"description\":\"Agent Package Manager\"}},\"additions\":502,\"deletions\":6,\"changed_files\":5,\"commits\":7,\"comments\":2,\"created_at\":\"2026-06-08T05:28:57Z\",\"updated_at\":\"2026-06-09T08:40:57Z\"}","type":"text"}]}}}
